---
title: "Marginalia"
subtitle: "Context-Aware RAG for Agricultural Decision Support"
author: "Zeno F."
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, marginalia-theme.scss]
    transition: fade
    transition-speed: slow
    slide-number: c/t
    progress: true
    logo: ""
    footer: "Marginalia · Interview Project"
    highlight-style: github
    code-block-bg: true
    code-block-border-left: "#3E7F6A"
    incremental: false
    width: 1200
    height: 700
    margin: 0.08
    auto-animate: true
    auto-animate-easing: ease-in-out
    auto-animate-duration: 0.6
engine: markdown
---

## {background-color="#F6F2E9" .title-custom}

::: {.title-block}
::: {.title-eyebrow}
Interview Project
:::

# Marginalia

::: {.title-subtitle}
Grounding LLM answers in domain knowledge and real-world data for agricultural decision support.
:::

::: {.title-meta}
Zeno F. · Shortlisted Interview · 2026
:::
:::

::: {.title-deco}
:::

---

## The Problem {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}

::: {.problem-block}
**Agricultural decisions need grounded, context-aware answers.**
Generic LLMs are not enough.
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.pain-points}
- LLMs hallucinate on domain-specific agronomic questions
- pure RAG answers "what do the documents say" — not "what should I do *today*"
- real decisions depend on both validated knowledge *and* real-time conditions
- outputs need traceability: sources, pages, citations
:::
:::

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
::: {.side-quote}
*"The rush to adopt AI without grounding it in validated science, local context, and institutional workflows often results in systems that are impressive in demonstrations but limited in field impact."*

— Shaik N. Meera, *From RAG and AI Agents to Context-Aware Agricultural Intelligence Systems*, LinkedIn, 2025
:::
:::
:::

---

## What is Marginalia? {auto-animate=true}

::: {.definition-card}
Marginalia is a **context-aware RAG agent** for agricultural knowledge bases. It combines structure-aware document ingestion, semantic retrieval, and a LangGraph agent that decides when to call external tools — starting with real-time weather data.
:::

::: {.columns .feature-row}
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.feature-pill}
📄 **Ingest**
Docling parsing with metadata
:::
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.feature-pill}
🧠 **Retrieve**
Embeddings, Qdrant, reranking
:::
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
::: {.feature-pill}
🤖 **Agent**
LangGraph, tool routing, citations
:::
:::
:::

---

## Architecture {auto-animate=true}

```text
PDF
→ Docling parsing
→ structured chunks + metadata (page, section, chunk type)
→ sentence-transformers embeddings
→ Qdrant vector store

question
→ rewrite (query reformulation with chat history)
→ tool_router  ──── does this need live data? ────┐
                                                   ▼
                               YES → weather_tool (Open-Meteo API)
                                                   │
                               NO ─────────────────┘
→ retrieve (Qdrant + cross-encoder reranking)
→ evidence blocks
→ answer (OpenAI, evidence + tool result as context)
→ cited sources
```

::: {.arch-note}
FastAPI exposes `/index` and `/ask`. The agent decides at runtime whether to call an external tool — the routing decision is made by the LLM itself, not by keyword matching.
:::

---

## Live Demo {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.definition-card}
**Document question**

*"What are best practices for drip irrigation?"*

→ tool router: **NO**
→ retrieves from agricultural knowledge base
→ grounded answer with citations
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.definition-card}
**Weather-dependent question**

*"What irrigation should I plan given this week's forecast?"*

→ tool router: **YES**
→ calls Open-Meteo API (7-day forecast)
→ answer combines document knowledge + live weather data
:::
:::
:::

::: {.arch-note}
The agent uses the LLM to decide which path to take — this is what makes it an agent rather than a fixed pipeline.
:::

---

## Results & Benchmarks {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.metric-card}
::: {.metric-number}
1.00
:::
::: {.metric-label}
Recall@5 on the retrieval benchmark
:::
:::

::: {.metric-card}
::: {.metric-number}
6
:::
::: {.metric-label}
LangGraph nodes in the QA agent workflow
:::
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.metric-card}
::: {.metric-number}
2
:::
::: {.metric-label}
API endpoints: `/index` and `/ask`
:::
:::

::: {.metric-card}
::: {.metric-number}
1
:::
::: {.metric-label}
External tool integrated: Open-Meteo weather API
:::
:::
:::
:::

---

## What I Learned {auto-animate=true}

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.learned-block .fragment}
**Document structure matters**
Preserving section, page, and chunk-type metadata is what makes grounded, traceable answers possible.
:::

::: {.learned-block .fragment}
**Retrieval quality matters as much as the LLM**
Query rewriting and cross-encoder reranking noticeably improve evidence quality before generation.
:::
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.learned-block .fragment}
**Agents need explicit routing logic**
A fixed pipeline cannot adapt to different question types. Explicit tool-routing nodes make the decision transparent and testable.
:::

::: {.learned-block .fragment}
**Evaluation keeps the system honest**
Fluent answers alone are not enough to judge a RAG pipeline — `recall@5` shows whether the right chunks are actually being retrieved.
:::
:::
:::

---

## What's Next {auto-animate=true}

::: {.roadmap}
::: {.roadmap-item .done}
✅ Ingestion, indexing, retrieval, grounded QA API
:::
::: {.roadmap-item .done}
✅ Reranking, query rewriting, request-level memory
:::
::: {.roadmap-item .done}
✅ LangGraph agent with weather tool routing
:::
::: {.roadmap-item .active}
🔄 Expose as an MCP server — any LLM agent can use this RAG as a tool
:::
::: {.roadmap-item}
⬜ Additional tools: crop disease lookup, soil condition APIs
:::
::: {.roadmap-item}
⬜ Persistent conversation memory across sessions
:::
:::

::: {.cta-block}
The same architecture scales to any agricultural knowledge base — swap the documents, extend the tools.
:::
